Supercut · creative playbook

coffee

What the longest-running paid social video ads in this market keep doing — counted, with the footage to prove it.

2026-07-12 · 56 ads studied across 49 brands

How we picked these ads

Advertisers kill losing ads fast, so an ad still delivering after a month has earned a look. We searched 240 ads in this market — including its heaviest spenders, so the pool reflects where real budgets go, not just what got views — and kept the ones running 30+ days and seen in the last 30. An AI watched each surviving ad and answered a fixed set of questions about it; code then counted the answers. 13 ads that turned out to sell something else were rejected — they are listed at the bottom. No single ad is called a winner here. The findings are patterns that repeat across 56 ads from 49 different brands.

What the studied ads sell

brewing equipment20 of 56
beans & pods11 of 56
mushroom coffee6 of 56
drinkware3 of 56
16 one-off segments16 of 56

Top takeaways

52 of 56Show the product in the first 3 seconds to match the market leaders.
31 of 56Use a creator-led feel to align with the majority of winning creative.
56 of 56Ensure all videos work without sound by using text overlays or clear visuals.
  1. Use visual hooks to demonstrate the product product demo · 20 ads
  2. Solve a specific problem using creators ugc problem-solution · 17 ads
  3. Bridge the gap between brand and product lifestyle montage · 8 ads
  4. Let creators walk through the full experience creator testimonial · 7 ads
  5. Lead with bold claims in static formats still image with text · 4 ads
  6. What to test next
  7. What to double-check

Chapter 1 · product demo

Use visual hooks to demonstrate the product

20 ads 19 brands 216.5 median days live 3.5 median cuts in first 10s 25s median length

This format lives on Facebook: 20 of 20 ads.

These ads rely heavily on seeing the product in action. Most ads in this group run on Facebook and use energetic or neutral tones.

The first three seconds, side by side

How these ads open

visual hook13 of 20
curiosity tease2 of 20
bold claim2 of 20
question1 of 20
social proof1 of 20
other1 of 20

When the product first appears

first 3 seconds20 of 20

How long they are

under 15s4 of 20
15-30s8 of 20
30-60s6 of 20
over 60s2 of 20

How they ask for the click

soft8 of 20
hard6 of 20
none6 of 20

The button under the ad

Shop now17 of 20
Learn more2 of 20
Order now1 of 20

How to shoot it

  • Show the product within the first 3 seconds. (20 of 20 ads)
  • Provide a full demonstration of the coffee process. (18 of 20 ads)
  • Use a visual hook to grab attention immediately. (13 of 20 ads)
  • Keep the pacing at a medium speed. (15 of 20 ads)

Examples that lasted

Lastaforest
165 days live · 135k plays · watch the ad
nothing.eco
Opens with: “HOT OR COLD?” · 161 days live · 398k plays · watch the ad
Wrappiness Personalized Gifts
Opens with: “I SMILE BECAUSE YOU'RE MY DAUGHTER I LAUGH BECAUSE THERE'S NOTHING YOU CAN DO ABOUT IT!” · 215 days live · 3.1M plays · watch the ad

Watch out

Only 1 ad in this group uses a question as a hook style.

  • Utilize a soft call to action. (5 of 7 ads)

Chapter 5 · still image with text

Lead with bold claims in static formats

4 ads 4 brands 230 median days live 0 median cuts in first 10s 9s median length

This format lives on Facebook: 4 of 4 ads.

Only 4 ads fit this format — read this chapter as a hint, not a rule.

These are short, static-based ads that work exclusively on Facebook. They are often the most long-running ads in the pool.

The first three seconds, side by side

How these ads open

bold claim1 of 4
offer or discount1 of 4
visual hook1 of 4
social proof1 of 4

When the product first appears

first 3 seconds4 of 4

How long they are

under 15s4 of 4

How they ask for the click

hard2 of 4
none2 of 4

The button under the ad

Shop now2 of 4
Order now2 of 4

How to shoot it

  • Show the product for the entire duration. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Keep the length under 15 seconds. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Ensure the feel is brand-focused. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Skip the product demonstration entirely. (4 of 4 ads)

Watch out

What to test next

Visual Hook vs. Problem Callout

Hypothesis
If we lead with a visual hook instead of a problem callout, we will see higher initial engagement.
Why this test
Visual hooks are the most common in the pool (26 out of 56 ads) compared to problem callouts (8 out of 56 ads).
Call it on
3-second view rate
Shot notes
  • Record a close-up of coffee pouring.
  • Include text that states a bold claim.
  • Show the product brand clearly.
Risk
The visual may be too generic and blend into the feed.
Draft script
Watch this perfect pour. The best morning routine starts right here. Get yours now.

Creator Pacing Challenge

Hypothesis
If we use fast pacing in a creator testimonial, we will increase completion rates.
Why this test
Every creator testimonial in the pool currently uses medium pacing (7 out of 7 ads).
Call it on
Average view duration
Shot notes
  • Use quick cuts between the creator speaking.
  • Show a full demo but in fast motion.
  • Overlay high-energy background music.
Risk
The fast speed may make the testimonial feel less sincere.
Draft script
I found the secret to better mornings. It's clean, it's fast, and it tastes amazing. You need to try this.

Offer-Led Static Text

Hypothesis
If we lead with a discount hook on static images, we will drive higher click-through rates than social proof.
Why this test
Only 2 ads in the entire pool lead with an offer or discount hook style.
Call it on
Outbound click-through rate
Shot notes
  • Place large '50% Off' text over a high-res product photo.
  • Keep the logo in the top corner.
  • Use a 'Shop Now' button style.
Risk
A heavy focus on price might devalue the brand's aesthetic.
Draft script
Get 50% off your first order. The coffee you love, cheaper. Shop now.

Sample brand brief

This section shows the brief generator on a made-up brand: “Bloom, a small-batch mushroom coffee brand for busy parents”. In the app, you type your own brand and the tests below are rewritten for it.

Bloom is a small-batch mushroom coffee brand designed to support the demanding schedules of busy parents.

Bloom can dominate by combining high-engagement visual hooks with the efficiency of mushroom coffee. Since the market heavily favors demonstrating the product early, we should focus on high-energy visuals that communicate parent-specific benefits immediately.

Visual Hook Morning Routine

The idea
A high-energy visual sequence showing the Bloom coffee pour transitioned into a parent successfully managing a chaotic morning. It prioritizes immediate visual proof of the product's small-batch quality.
The pattern it leans on
Visual hooks are the dominant entry point in the market, used in 26 out of 56 ads.
Opening line
“This perfect pour is a busy parent's secret weapon.”
Draft script
[Visual: Slow-motion pour of Bloom coffee into a mug]. Narrator: Watch this perfect pour. [Visual: Parent smiling while kids play in background]. Narrator: The best morning routine starts right here. Sustained focus without the crash. [Visual: Bloom bag on counter]. Narrator: Get your mushroom coffee now via the link below.

Fast-Paced Parent Testimonial

The idea
A creator-led video featuring a busy mom or dad explaining why they switched to Bloom, edited with rapid-fire cuts. This disrupts the current market trend of slower-paced testimonials.
The pattern it leans on
Every creator testimonial in the market currently uses medium pacing with 7 out of 7 ads following this trend.
Opening line
“I found the secret to surviving the school run.”
Draft script
[Visual: Quick cuts of creator nodding, sipping, and packing bags]. Creator: I found the secret to better mornings. It's clean, it's fast, and it tastes amazing. [Visual: Fast demo of mixing Bloom]. Creator: No jitters, just pure parent power. You need to try this mushroom coffee. Shop Bloom today.

Direct Savings Static Image

The idea
A clean, high-resolution photo of Bloom's small-batch packaging with a large, bold discount overlaying the product. This targets price-conscious parents who need an immediate reason to switch from standard beans.
The pattern it leans on
Only 2 ads in the entire pool of 56 lead with an offer or discount hook style.
Opening line
“Get 50% off your first batch of Bloom.”
Draft script
[Static Image: Bloom mushroom coffee bag next to a finished cup. Text Overlay: 50% OFF YOUR FIRST ORDER]. Narrator: Get 50% off your first order. Professional grade mushroom coffee, at a price choice parents love. Shop now and save.

What to double-check

Everything above comes from an AI watching ads one at a time and code counting its answers. Here is where that process was noisy, and what it threw out.

Ads rejected as off-market (13)

The search also returned ads selling something else. They were dropped before any counting, so they do not touch the numbers above.

AdvertiserWhy it was dropped
Clean Simple Eatsoff-market: sells "Clean Simple Eats vanilla protein powder for use in coffee recipes."
Becoming the Ultimate Human with Gary Breckaoff-market: sells "A dietary supplement called PerfectAmino that is added to coffee to provide essential amino acids."
Site To Buyoff-market: sells "A service that sets up a private-label coffee dropshipping store for you."
Site To Buyoff-market: sells "A service that builds custom Shopify coffee dropshipping stores for entrepreneurs."
ShopFlavCityoff-market: sells "A coffee-flavored protein smoothie powder."
Sophie's dealsoff-market: sells "A hardtop gazebo for outdoor use."
Because I Love Youoff-market: sells "A personalized 'Pocket Hug' keepsake token with a leather keychain case."
Luna's Mushroomsoff-market: sells "Lion's Mane mushroom powder, a dietary supplement to be added to beverages like coffee."
Go Brewingoff-market: sells "A non-alcoholic coffee stout beer."
FocusBlinkoff-market: sells "Mushroom-based focus strips containing Lion's Mane as a caffeine-free alternative to coffee."
Luke the PT Dadoff-market: sells "A portable home sauna that uses infrared and red light therapy to boost health and energy."
addallxroff-market: sells "Addall XR, a dietary supplement marketed for mental focus and energy."
Vorioaloff-market: sells "Women's professional and casual outerwear and matching trousers."

How reliable was the watching?

We measured the editing pace of every ad twice: once by the AI's eye, once by scene-detection software. The AI was off by 2.2 cuts on average, and within 2 cuts of the software on 64% of ads. The cut counts printed in the chapters are the software's, not the AI's.

Where a spoken hook could be checked against the ad library's own transcript (18 ads), the AI's quote matched 18 times and disagreed 0 times. The other 38 ads had on-screen-text hooks, no hook, or no transcript — nothing to check against.

15 of 56 ads were described by the AI as making no ask, yet the ad unit itself carries a purchase button (ads f8eee66620c9efdca5c63, f3ceb491247f019424154, fe0ae602e19999a020122, fc17d5f377d00742215bc, fdf35298e62c9e0accb1b, f2713316a3b9459fd05bb, fce6969464bdd03ef3e5c, f88bd5494b53afcaab546, f0107e03d8097b7b641f5, f7b7f015392c915b8d6ff, f3363d3be4d9d65afc492, fb78aca847e59b2fca2e0, f4ffda667dbf9e12cee57, fabf7c7017e25d955cdc1, f6688c2287d7b3efda0a5). Read those "no ask" counts with that in mind: the AI judges the video, not the button under it.

The AI reported firm format labels for every ad in the pool.

Assumptions this report makes

  • "Still delivering" means the ad was seen in the ad library within the last 30 days. The data offers no direct "currently live" flag.
  • Days-running and play counts come from the ad library and are taken at face value.
  • A long-running ad earned study, but nothing here proves any single ad was profitable. Only the repeated patterns are conclusions.
  • Spoken quotes were transcribed by the AI from the audio; where the ad library had its own transcript, we cross-checked (see above).

Study ranking: ads are ordered for the example lists by a score of longevity ×0.5 + engagement ×0.3 + formatConfidence ×0.2, each part normalized within this pool, with at most one ad per brand in any list. Engagement is measured as a rate — likes and shares per play, a share counting 5× a like — because plays on a paid ad are bought, so raw counts would mostly rank budgets. Rates from under 10,000 plays are ignored as noise.

Made with Supercut. Days-running and play counts come from the ad library data; "still delivering" means seen in the last 30 days, the closest signal the data offers to "currently live".
